era_context: The height of Alexander the Great's Persian campaign. Following his victory at Gaugamela (331 BCE), Alexander is consolidating control over the eastern satrapies of the Achaemenid Empire. In Greece, the Macedonian hegemony under Antipater remains firm. In China, the Warring States period continues with Qin ascendant. In India, the Nanda Empire dominates the Gangetic plain.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources for this period)
Northern Europe beyond Greek and Roman awareness
Detailed records from Qin China are sparse for this specific year

events.title: Alexander's Campaign in Bactria and Sogdiana
events.description: Alexander advances into Bactria and Sogdiana, pursuing the remnants of the Persian army under Bessus. He encounters fierce resistance from local populations and nomadic tribes. The campaign is marked by brutal sieges and the consolidation of Macedonian control over the northeastern reaches of the former Persian Empire. This expansion extends Alexander's supply lines and introduces his army to Central Asian warfare tactics.

sources.name: Curtius Rufus, History of Alexander the Great

events.certainty_note: No contemporary written records survive. Dating relies on Arrian's chronology, which is generally reliable but has internal inconsistencies regarding the exact year of events in Bactria-Sogdiana. Modern scholarship places this campaign between 330–327 BCE with uncertainty of ±1–2 years.

events.title: Capture and Execution of Bessus
events.description: Bessus, the last major Persian resistance leader and satrap of Bactria, is captured by Alexander's forces. Bessus had proclaimed himself Artaxerxes V after Darius III's death. He is executed—accounts vary on whether by crucifixion or flaying—marking the symbolic end of organized Persian resistance. This event consolidates Alexander's claim to be the legitimate successor to the Achaemenid throne.

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book III–IV
Curtius Rufus, History of Alexander the Great, Book VII

events.certainty_note: Dating of Bessus's capture is debated. Arrian places it in 329 BCE, but some scholars argue for 328 BCE. The execution itself is confirmed in tradition, though details of the method vary across sources.

events.title: Antipater Suppresses Agis III's Rebellion in Greece
events.description: Antipater, regent of Macedon, defeats Agis III of Sparta in the Battle of Megalopolis. Agis had attempted to lead a Greek revolt against Macedonian hegemony while Alexander was distant in Asia. The rebellion is crushed, and Agis is killed. This victory secures Macedonian control over Greece and prevents any coordinated Greek resistance to Alexander's rule.

sources.name: Diodorus Siculus, Bibliotheca Historica, Book XVII

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Megalopolis is well-attested in multiple ancient sources. Dating to 331 BCE is more commonly accepted by modern scholarship, but some sources place it in 330 or 329 BCE. The event itself is historically certain.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Bessus's capture is disputed. While Arrian places it in 329 BCE, modern scholars including some in the Bosworth school argue for 328 BCE based on the chronology of Spitamenes' rebellion and Alexander's movements. The Battle of Megalopolis is often dated to 331 BCE rather than 329 BCE in recent scholarship, which would place it outside this year. The exact sequence and dating of Alexander's Bactrian campaign remains contested due to the absence of contemporary sources and inconsistencies in later accounts.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 329 BCE is entirely dependent on later Greek and Roman compilations, primarily Arrian (2nd century CE) and Curtius Rufus (1st century CE), who drew on earlier sources now lost. No contemporary written records from Alexander's court survive. The accounts are shaped by the perspective of Alexander's companions and later Hellenistic historians. Persian sources are absent. The chronology of Alexander's Central Asian campaigns is notoriously difficult to establish precisely, and modern scholars debate the exact year-by-year sequence. Archaeological evidence from Central Asia for this period is limited. The record is heavily weighted toward military and political events; economic, social, and cultural developments in conquered territories are largely invisible.

graph_edges.note: Bactrian campaign continues into 328 BCE with Spitamenes' rebellion
Bessus's capture is consequence of Alexander's victory at Gaugamela
Suppression of Greek resistance continues Macedonian consolidation
